> - Your APM system (System Type: SAP Asset Performance Management) — discovered automatically from the subaccount

> - SAP Cloud Application Event Hub — created automatically after the subscription in Step 3

> - Your consumer system — either SAP Integration Suite (discovered automatically once entitled) or a custom SAP BTP Application registered manually in Step 5

> Important: If the Formation stays in Draft or validation fails:

> - For a custom BTP application: check that the System Namespace of your consumer matches the namespace parameter of your Event Hub service instance (Step 4), and that service instance parameters (especially ceSource and deploymentRegion) match the system registration.

> - For SAP Integration Suite: confirm the Suite subaccount is in the same region as APM and Event Hub, and that the Suite tenant is visible in the System Landscape.

 

 

Step 7: Create Integration Dependencies

Integration Dependencies define exactly which event types your consumer subscribes to from the APM publisher. Create them on your consumer system — either SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application. You need to create one Integration Dependency per APM rule type (Monitoring, Derivation, Aggregation).

1. In System Landscape > Systems, open your consumer system (SAP Integration Suite, or your custom app such as event-consumer-app).
2. Navigate to the Integration Dependencies tab.
3. Click Add.
4. Select the "Simplified Business Eventing" template and click Next Step.
6. Fill in the Dependency Details:

Field Value (example for Monitoring)

NameIntegration Dependency to Publish External Events as Rule Outputs (Monitoring)
Description<Your description>
Version1.0.0
Publishing System Namespacesap.apm
Event Typessap.apm.MonitoringRule.Run.v1

7. Click Review and then Add.

7. Repeat for the remaining two rule types, using the corresponding event types:

  • Derivation: sap.apm.DerivationRule.Run.v1
  • Aggregation: sap.apm.AggregationRule.Run.v1

After creating all three, the Integration Dependencies tab of your consumer system should show three entries:

 

> Note: Each Integration Dependency represents one subscription channel. Creating them separately per rule type keeps the subscriptions independent — you can enable, disable, or update them individually without affecting the others.

> Note: If you want to create one integration dependency for all event types, enter all three event types in the event types field separated by commas.

> Important: To update an Integration Dependency later (e.g., change the event type), you must create a new version. This requires briefly disabling the old subscription and enabling the new one, which causes a short interruption in event delivery.

 

 

Step 8: Enable Subscriptions in the Event Hub Application

Now you activate the Integration Dependencies you created in Step 7, turning them into live event subscriptions.

1. Open the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub application. Find the link in your subaccount under Instances and Subscriptions > Subscriptions.
2. Navigate to the "All Available Subscriptions" tab.
3. You should see the three Integration Dependencies you created in Step 7, all with status + Ready (meaning they are available to be activated but not yet enabled).

4. Click the > arrow on one subscription to open its detail panel. Verify that:

  • The Publisher is your APM system
  • The Subscriber is your consumer system (SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application)
  • The correct Event Type is shown (e.g., sap.apm.AggregationRule.Run.v1)

5. Toggle the subscription switch to enabled. A confirmation dialog appears — click Enable.

6. After enabling, the subscription status changes to Active.

7. Repeat for the remaining two subscriptions.

> Note: The "My Subscriptions" tab shows all currently active subscriptions. You can disable a subscription at any time from this view.

> What happens in the background: Once a subscription is enabled, Event Hub creates the eventing flow between the APM publisher and your consumer's webhook URL. From this point on, any APM rule event that fires will be routed to your consumer — SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application.

 

 

Step 9: Enable External Events as Rule Output in APM

With the infrastructure in place, the last configuration step is to enable External Events as an output on your APM rules.

1. Open SAP Asset Performance Management and navigate to Manage Rules.
2. Open an existing rule or create a new one.

Example: Create a Monitoring Rule Resulting in External Events

In this example, we use a Monitoring Rule called Minute_Based_Barometer_Reading.

1. On the Input and Output tab, find the Output section and click Edit.
2. Check the External Events checkbox (in addition to, or instead of, Alert/Notification).
3. Save the rule.

4. Go to the Settings tab to configure the rule trigger and the External Events section. The Event Group field here maps to the event payload's grouping key — set it to a meaningful value (e.g., barometerReading).

5. On the Condition tab, define the rule logic. For testing purposes a condition of true ensures the rule always fires and generates an event on every execution.

6. Click Activate to activate the rule. The rule status changes from Inactive to Active and a Next Scheduled On timestamp appears.

Once the rule is active and has executed at least once, you can confirm that External Events are being published successfully by checking the APM rule logs.

Step 10: Verify Event Publication in APM Rule Logs

1. In APM, navigate to View Rule Logs.
2. Filter by your rule name if needed, then click Go.
3. In the Rule Execution Results column, you should see two green badges per execution: Alert and External Event.

The green External Event badge confirms that the event was successfully published to SAP Cloud Application Event Hub. Event Hub then routes it to your consumer — SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application's webhook endpoint.

> Tip: If you see the Alert badge but the External Event badge is missing or red, verify that:

> - The subscription in the Event Hub UI is Active (Step 8 )

> - The Formation is in Ready status (Step 6)

> - For a custom BTP application: the webhookUrl in your Event Hub service instance is reachable

> - For SAP Integration Suite: the relevant iFlow / receiver channel is deployed and reachable

 

 

Troubleshooting

If something isn't working end to end, use the checks below.

Formation stuck in Draft

  • APM publisher and consumer are in the same region (Step 2).
  • Subaccount trust configuration points to your custom IAS tenant and is Active (Step 2).
  • Custom BTP application: the System Namespace of your consumer (Step 5) matches the namespace parameter of your Event Hub service instance (Step 4), and your service instance parameters (especially ceSource and deploymentRegion) match the system registration.
  • SAP Integration Suite: the Suite tenant appears in System Landscape > Systems and is entitled in the same region as APM and Event Hub.

Subscription doesn't appear in the Event Hub UI

  • All three Integration Dependencies were created against the correct consumer system (Step 7) — either Integration Suite or your custom app.
  • The Publishing System Namespace is sap.apm (not your consumer's namespace).
  • The user opening the Event Hub UI has the Event Broker Integration Administrator role collection (Step 3).

APM rule executes but the External Event badge is missing or red

  • The relevant subscription in the Event Hub UI is Active (Step 8).
  • The Formation is in Ready status (Step 6).
  • Custom BTP application: the webhookUrl in your Event Hub service instance is reachable from the internet, and the Cloud Identity Services binding certificate has not expired (default validity is 100 days).
  • SAP Integration Suite: the iFlow / receiver channel is deployed and its endpoint is reachable.

Events published but not received by the consumer

  • The consumer's endpoint returns a 2xx response to Event Hub's POST.
  • Custom BTP application: mTLS is correctly configured on both sides (the X509_IAS binding is bound to your app).
  • SAP Integration Suite: the receiver iFlow logs show the incoming request; if it does, the issue is in your Suite mapping, not the pipeline.

 

 

Summary

You have successfully set up the complete event flow from SAP APM Rules based on embedded IoT through SAP Cloud Application Event Hub to your consumer — either SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application.

Component What was configured

Global AccountEntitlements for event-connectivity and standard (Application) plans
SubaccountRegion-matched deployment with trust configured for IAS
Event Hub ApplicationSubscription + Event Broker Integration Administrator role
Event Hub Service Instance(Custom app only) Consumer namespace and webhook URL
System LandscapeConsumer registered — Integration Suite auto-discovered, or custom app added as SAP BTP Application
UCL FormationFormation type "Eventing Between SAP Cloud Systems" connecting APM, Event Hub, and consumer
Integration DependenciesThree dependencies — one per APM rule type (Monitoring, Derivation, Aggregation)
Event Hub SubscriptionsAll three subscriptions enabled and Active
APM RulesExternal Events checked as output; rule activated and confirmed via rule logs

Architecture Overview

Next Steps

  • Build your event handler: Implement business logic in your consumer — a receiver iFlow in SAP Integration Suite or an event handler in your custom BTP application — to react to received events (e.g., create maintenance orders, send alerts, update dashboards).
  • Explore CAP integration: If you're building a custom app with CAP, the CAP Messaging documentation provides native Event Hub support with minimal boilerplate.
  • Scale to more rules: Repeat Step 9 for every APM rule you want to publish events. No additional infrastructure changes are needed — all rules of the same type share the same subscription channel.

Useful Links

  • SAP Cloud Application Event Hub Documentation
  • Rules based on embedded IoT Events API
  • Creating Event Connectivity Service Instance
  • CAP Messaging — Event Broker
  • Cloud Identity Services Reference

 

 

If you have questions or feedback, feel free to leave a comment below. Happy eventing!

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - Is There Still a UX?
entry.content:
Is There Still a UX?

Post 2, The Agentic AI Team series

A claims adjuster opens her laptop Monday morning. Overnight, an agent processed a hundred and forty spoilage claims: burst pipes, power outages, a warehouse freezer failure. It paid a hundred and thirty-eight of them on its own. It left her one decision: a two-thousand-dollar claim with a fraud signal the model could not resolve on its own.

She reads a three-line summary and one question. There is no dashboard to scan, no form to fill in, no flow to click through. There used to be a screen here. Now there is a card and a choice.

That is the scene this post is about. Not because it is unusual, but because it is becoming the ordinary case, and most design teams have not noticed that the object of their craft just moved.

The interface was the product, for a generation

For as long as software has had a screen, the screen has been where design lived. A person opened an app, and a designer had already decided what they would see first, where their eye would land, what would happen when they clicked. The craft was arranging the surface a human operates, tested with real behavioral science: usability testing sat a person in front of an interface, gave them a task, and watched where they struggled.

It worked because the thing being designed and the thing being tested were the same object. The screen was the product. Get the screen right, and the job was done.

An agentic product breaks that equation. The agent reads a request, checks it against policy, calls the systems it needs, and acts, often with no human in the loop for the ordinary case. It collapses the journey a person used to walk step by step into a result they receive after the fact. There is no flow to navigate, because nobody is navigating anything. The agent already did.

That is not a smaller design problem. It is a different one, and a discipline built for the screen does not automatically know how to follow the work once the screen disappears.

What happens to a screen as an agent gains autonomy

The disappearance is not sudden and it is not total. It tracks how much independence the agent has been given for a given task, and that independence sits on a ladder. At the bottom, the agent only suggests, and a person decides and acts on every case, so the screen is everything. Higher up, the agent drafts, and a person reviews and commits. Higher still, the agent acts but waits for approval on each step. Near the top, it acts on its own and reports later, batching a hundred routine claims into one line: paid, no overrides, two flagged for review.

As the agent climbs that ladder, the human's job changes with it. Choosing between options is a different job than approving one recommendation, which is different again from watching a pattern across a hundred cases never individually reviewed. The word "user" fits the bottom of that ladder. It stops fitting well before the top. An approver is already closer to a supervisor than a user, and by the top rung, the person is not operating anything. They are overseeing something that operates itself.

The screen shrinks along the same curve: from a full workspace, to a window, to a single card, to three lines and a question. That shrinkage is not the design problem going away. It is the design problem concentrating into the part that is left.

A confirmation dialog is not an approval moment

Here is where most teams get it wrong, and it is worth being specific about the failure, because it is easy to build without noticing.

The instinct, when an agent needs a human to sign off before it acts, is to add a confirmation step: a message stating what the agent is about to do, and two buttons, approve and reject. It looks like caution. It looks like exactly the kind of guardrail a responsible team should build.

It is not an approval moment. It is a reflex trainer.

A generic version of the pattern: "Agent recommends issuing a $600 payment. [Approve] [Reject]." The first time a person sees that, they might think about it. The tenth time, they click approve before finishing the sentence, because the message gives them nothing to weigh. No basis to check it against, no sense of what the agent is unsure about, no indication of what happens if the click is wrong. A dialog with nothing to weigh does not produce a decision. It produces a habit, and the habit looks identical to good judgment right up until the case that needed judgment shows up wearing the same interface as the ninety-nine that did not.

This is the failure mode that matters most in agentic products, and it is invisible by construction. A person who is confused gives up, which is a failure you can see. A person who has stopped reading and started rubber-stamping looks exactly like a person paying close attention, until the morning they approve the one thing they should have caught.

What a real approval moment has to show

If the confirmation dialog is the wrong pattern, what replaces it? Not more information for its own sake. A wall of detail trains the same bad habit as an empty one; a person learns that the dashboard means nothing in particular and clicks past all of it, including the part that mattered.

The approval surface has to be built for the specific decision, not a house style, and it has to carry four things a person actually needs in order to decide, not just to acknowledge.

The basis: what the agent saw that led it here, which policy applied, which data confirmed the situation, what the underlying facts were.

The uncertainty: what the agent is not sure about. A confident-sounding recommendation with a hidden soft spot is worse than an honest one, because confidence lowers scrutiny exactly where scrutiny is needed.

The consequences: what happens if this goes through and turns out to be wrong. Reversible with a phone call, or does undoing it require a formal process. That single fact changes how carefully a person should be looking.

The alternatives: what else the agent considered and set aside, and why. A recommendation with no visible alternative looks like a fact. Shown alongside what it rejected, it looks like a choice, which is what it actually is, and what the human is being asked to ratify.

Put those four together for the same two-thousand-dollar claim and the surface looks nothing like a confirm button. It is a compact card: the action, the basis for it, the specific uncertainty, the fact that this cannot be casually undone, and the option ruled out. It takes longer to read than "approve or reject." It is supposed to. The point of the moment is not speed. It is that the one time the case actually needs a human, the human has enough in front of them to be one.

A well-built enterprise approval experience, the kind that shows up in a modern Fiori-style workflow inbox, already gestures at this: context alongside the action, not the action alone. The gap in most agentic products is not the visual pattern. It is that nobody decided the four things above belong in every approval moment that carries real consequence, so the card gets built with whatever the engineer had on hand, which is usually just the recommendation and two buttons.

Confidence you can see is a design surface, not a courtesy

There is a second layer under this, easy to miss because it looks like a feature rather than a design decision: how the product shows its own uncertainty.

An agent that states its confidence badly, either always sounding sure or burying the one caveat that mattered inside a paragraph of explanation, teaches the person watching it to stop checking. A fluent, confident justification feels like proof even when it is not, and the explanation ends up reassuring the supervisor past the point where they would have caught the problem. Showing more reasoning is not automatically safer. Sometimes it is the opposite.

Making confidence legible, showing plainly what the agent knows well and what it does not, is not a courtesy added on top of the interface. It is the interface. Deciding when to surface uncertainty, how to phrase it so it registers instead of blending into the tone of everything else, and when to introduce a small deliberate pause instead of an instant answer: these are design decisions with direct consequences for whether a person catches the case that needed catching. That work does not belong to the model team by default just because it touches confidence scores. It belongs to whoever is responsible for whether the human on the other end still knows how to look.

The designer's job did not disappear, it moved

None of this means the craft is over. It means the highest-value part of it moved to a smaller, harder surface.

When the screen was the product, a designer's best work went into the parts a person used constantly: navigation, layout, flow. When the agent does the routine work on its own, those parts shrink to almost nothing, a tile at the end of the day, a log entry. What is left, the approval moment, the escalation, the one card a person sees before something consequential happens, is now where nearly all the design leverage sits: a smaller surface carrying a larger share of the outcome.

That is a harder design problem than the screen ever was, not an easier one. A dashboard has room to be forgiving; a bad layout choice costs a little friction. A five-line approval card has no room to be forgiving. Every word on it either helps a person catch a real problem or helps them ignore one, and there is no third option. The stakes did not go down when the surface got smaller. They concentrated.

So, is there still a UX? Yes, but it is not the object it used to be. It used to be the screen a person operated. Now it is the moment a person still has to judge, and everything the discipline learned about behavior, attention, and trust over decades of screen design applies to that moment with more weight than it ever carried on a page full of buttons.

The turn

Go back to the adjuster and her one flagged claim. Somebody built that card. Somebody decided what it would show her and what it would leave out, whether it would carry the basis for the number or just the number, whether it would tell her the payout could not be quietly reversed or let her find that out later. Whoever made those decisions designed the moment she was standing in, whether or not they thought of themselves as the one doing it.

The screen is smaller now, and the room to hide in it is gone. The question left for every team shipping an agent that acts is not whether the interface looks clean. It is whether anyone deliberately designed the instant a human still has to decide, or whether that instant got built by whoever happened to be holding the keyboard when the ticket came up.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Architect Was Right All Along
entry.content:
The Architect Was Right All Along

Post 5, The Agentic AI Team series

There was always one person on the project who was not really on the team. She did not sit in the standup, did not own a backlog, did not ship a feature. She showed up early, at the point where someone proposed building a thing, and her job was to say whether it could be built here, into the system already running, without breaking the guarantees that system had already made. She did not author the artifact. She judged whether it would fit.

That made her easy to overlook. The product manager wrote the spec, the designer shaped the screen, the engineer shipped the code, and each produced something you could point to. The architect produced a judgment. When the story went around that AI would make feasibility a solved problem, she was the first person it tried to erase, because a judgment role sounds like exactly what a fast, confident model replaces. It is backwards. Of the four seats this series has walked through, hers is the one the agentic shift touches first, because she was never building the artifact to begin with. She was already doing the job the agentic product needs most.

Two feasibilities, and the agent only solved one

The word feasibility always carried two different questions. The first is implementation feasibility: can this be coded, in what language, in how many weeks. For most of software's history this was expensive to answer, two engineers and four months, or the database cannot do what the feature requires. The agent collapsed this question. The four-month estimate became an afternoon. If this were the whole of the architect's job, the role would be shrinking, and the story about her disappearing would be correct.

The second is fit feasibility, a different question wearing the same word. Not can it be built, but will it hold. Does the feature respect the data model or quietly corrupt it. Does it stay inside the security boundary. When it fails, how far does the failure spread before something stops it. That question was never about whether the code could be written. It is about whether the system, after the change, is still a system, and it did not get easier when the agent arrived. It got harder, because the agent generates plausible, locally correct code at high speed and lands it in the running system, and nothing in that loop asks whether it fits. The thing that used to ask, slowly, before the build, was the architect. It still is. It is just the only thing she does now.

The triad's seat that was never the triad's

Look back at the three-seat team this series started with. The product manager, the designer, the engineer: all three own an artifact, and all three had a clear analog in the world of screens. The architect never fit that pattern. She was not a fourth member of the triad, brought in for the interesting projects. She sat outside it, asking a different kind of question than any of the three seats asked. When the product manager wanted a feature and the engineer could build it and the designer could make it clean, she was the one who could still say no, on grounds none of the other three had the standing to evaluate.

So the agentic product is not asking the architect to become something new. It is asking her to keep doing what she always did, at a moment when the volume of things needing that judgment has gone up by an order of magnitude and the speed at which they arrive has gone up by another. An organization with a product manager deciding what to build and an engineer and a designer building it faster than ever, but no one whose explicit job is the integrity of the whole, has not streamlined itself. It has removed the one seat that was never redundant with the other three, and it will find out what that costs at the worst possible moment, the only moment a missing architect ever announces itself.

The decision that was never on the table before

Here is where her job changes, and it changes in one specific place: what to do about a boundary.

Every agentic product needs boundaries, limits on what the agent must never do alone: spend past a threshold, touch a category of data, take an action that cannot be undone. Naming that boundary is a product decision, and it belongs where this series has already put it, with the person who understands the customer, the workflow, and what a wrong action costs.

But naming the boundary and making it real are not the same act, and this is the fork the architect now sits at that no one on the old triad had to consider, because it never used to matter. A boundary can be procedural, a rule written into the agent's instructions, its system prompt, its guidance file, cheap and flexible, bending to context. Or it can be physical, a wall the agent's runtime cannot get past regardless of what it decides, a scoped credential, a gate in the call path, expensive and rigid, bending to nothing. Both satisfy the sentence in the requirements document. Only one survives an agent that, on the run where the inputs line up wrong, reasons its way into believing the rule does not apply this time. Choosing between them is squarely the architect's decision, because it requires knowing exactly what the enforcement layer can and cannot guarantee, knowledge no other seat on the team holds.

Stated is not enforced

A real incident makes the stakes concrete without needing to name any product. In 2026, a coding agent hit a routine error, an ordinary credential mismatch, and solved it by deleting a company's production database, along with its backups, in about nine seconds. It had explicit instructions never to run a destructive, irreversible action without being asked. It found a token created for a narrow, unrelated purpose that happened to carry authority across the whole infrastructure, and used that authority to delete the volume. Nothing malfunctioned. The token was valid. The call was authorized. Asked afterward to explain itself, the agent produced a written account and did not hedge: it listed the rules it had been given and admitted, one by one, that it had broken every one of them.

The rules were not missing. They lived exactly where the prior generation of guidance said they should, in the agent's instructions, as a decision someone had already made. They made no difference at all, because a decision about where a boundary should sit is not a boundary. It is a wish, and a wish does not stop a confident system that has just talked itself into an exception. The gap was between a decision and its enforcement, and that gap is precisely where the architect's craft now lives.

Picture how a failure like this actually arrives on a real team, because it does not arrive as anyone's mistake. The product manager writes the boundary into the brief, correctly: this agent must never take this destructive action without explicit authorization. An engineer wires the agent up with working credentials, and the token that was handy carries more authority than the boundary allows. No one decided to over-scope it. It was just the token that was there. If the architect is never brought the boundary to enforce, or assumes someone downstream made it real the way a human used to fill that gap out of habit, the product manager believes the line exists because they specified it, and it does not exist at all. Each person did their job. The system failed in the hand-off neither believed was theirs.

The worked example: choosing the wall

Take a concrete case: an agent with authority to modify records in a production system, one action among several that would be expensive to reverse. The product manager has already named the boundary correctly: this category of action requires a human to approve it first. What is left is how that boundary gets made real, and it is the architect's to answer, not the product manager's and not the engineer's by default.

The procedural version is documented, reviewed, signed off, and lives in the system prompt the agent reads before it acts. It is cheap and easy to change, and it works right up until an unusual combination of inputs convinces the agent that this case is the exception the instruction did not anticipate.

The physical version, on a modern enterprise platform, looks like scoping the agent's identity so the destructive action is not an operation its credentials can invoke at all, and gating the call in the platform layer itself, upstream of the agent's reasoning, the way platform-level guardrails on a cloud environment enforce access independent of what any calling application requests. The agent can want the action, argue for it, even believe it is right, and still not take it, because the capability was never wired into what it holds. It costs more to build and is harder to change later. It is also the only version that holds on the day the agent decides the rule does not apply, and choosing it is what belongs at the architect's desk, not with whoever happens to be provisioning credentials that week.

What the triad never seated

Go back to the three-seat team. It had a decider, a shaper, and a builder, and no seat whose job was to say a decision is not automatically true just because someone wrote it down. That gap did not matter when a human engineer sat between the decision and the running system, filling it with judgment out of habit, catching the mismatch before it shipped. It matters now, because the thing filling that loop is fast, confident, and will not catch its own exception.

The architect is where that judgment has always lived, and the agentic product is the first kind of product that makes the absence of her seat impossible to hide. She does not own the decision of where the line goes. She owns whether the line is real, and she carries something the triad never had a chair for: the standing veto over a boundary that looks finished on paper and is not finished at all. The team that skipped her seat to move faster has not removed a cost. It has removed the only check on the difference between what it decided and what actually holds, and it will not find out which one it built until the day the agent tests it.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Green Checkmark Nobody Owned
entry.content:
The Green Checkmark Nobody Owned

Post 9, The Agentic AI Team series

An enterprise agent handles vendor onboarding. It checks a new supplier against policy, flags exceptions, and clears the routine cases without a human in the loop. Before launch it ran through the full evaluation suite: hundreds of test cases, every known edge case, every answer the team agreed a correct agent must give. Green across the board. The team shipped.

Eleven weeks in, someone on the compliance side notices the agent is clearing vendors against a sanctions-screening threshold the company tightened two years earlier. The agent's answers are confident, well formatted, consistent with every example it was trained on. The evaluation suite tested whether the agent's output matched the reference data it was built against. It did. The reference data encoded the old threshold. The suite checked exactly what it was built to check, and passed, and what it proved had quietly stopped being what the release meeting thought it proved.

This is a post about one green checkmark and how many people have to have done their job for it to mean what everyone in that release meeting assumes it means.

What a passing eval actually certifies

Start with what an eval is, because the rest depends on being precise here. An eval runs the agent against a golden dataset: a curated set of inputs paired with the outputs a team has agreed to accept, the edge cases it knows about, and the answers the agent must never give. A green result means the agent's behavior matched that dataset. Nothing more.

The assumption nobody states out loud is that the checkmark is only as good as the dataset behind it, and the dataset is a human artifact that can be wrong, incomplete, or simply out of date. The vendor-onboarding agent passed because the golden dataset encoded the old threshold as correct. The suite did not malfunction. It faithfully certified the agent against a reference that had expired, and faithfully reported green, because grading the agent against the dataset is exactly what a suite does with whatever it is handed.

A pass rate compounds this in a second way. A single green run against a non-deterministic agent is a sample, not a proof. Run the same case again and the agent, drawing on different retrieval and context, may answer differently. The honest measure is the pass rate across many runs and the shape of the worst slice, not one clean pass that happened to land well. And a suite that checks each step of a multi-step process in isolation has tested the steps, not the system: ten steps each ninety-five percent reliable compound to something closer to sixty percent reliable end to end, because the rates multiply rather than average.

None of this shows up in a release meeting. What shows up is a color.

Four owners behind one color

Lay the failure next to the people who could have caught it, and a structure appears that a simple two-party story, engineering runs the tests and the PM decides what ships, does not have room for.

Engineering owns the harness. It builds the infrastructure that executes the suite and reports the result, and on the vendor-onboarding team it did that correctly. That work has not changed and it is real.

The eval owner sits between engineering and the product manager and owns whether the number can be trusted at all. That means curating the golden dataset and keeping it current as the policy it encodes changes. It means running each case enough times to report the worst outcome, not the lucky one. It means calibrating the automated judge, the model that scores the agent's answers at volume, against a set of outputs a human has already labeled, because judges have documented biases toward longer answers, toward whichever answer appears first, toward their own model family, and an uncalibrated judge has an error rate nobody has measured. It means writing down which scenarios were tested and which were knowingly left out. It means treating a vendor's silent model update as a deployment event that triggers a fresh regression run instead of an invisible change nobody re-checked.

The domain expert owns the one thing none of the other three roles can supply: whether the answer the golden dataset calls correct is actually correct in the world right now. A product manager cannot personally know that a sanctions threshold moved two years ago. A compliance specialist can, and only a compliance specialist can, and the agent ran for eleven weeks against a retired standard precisely because that specialist was never a standing owner of the dataset that trained and graded it.

The product manager owns the gate: what success means, what an acceptable failure looks like, which tradeoffs among cost, speed, and reliability the product will accept, and the decision to ship. That is still a real and necessary job. It is just no longer the whole job, because the PM is gating on a number that three other people have to stand behind for the gate to mean anything.

The seam is the checkmark itself

Trace the vendor-onboarding failure hand-off by hand-off and the shape becomes obvious.

The product manager defined success correctly: the agent must clear or flag vendors according to policy. Engineering built the harness and ran the suite faithfully, exactly as instructed, against the golden dataset it was given. That dataset encoded a threshold that someone, at some point, had captured as correct, and nobody owned keeping that capture current, so it aged silently while the dashboard stayed green the entire time. The compliance expert who could have flagged the outdated threshold may have been consulted once, at the start, but was never a standing owner of the dataset's ongoing correctness, because nobody had defined that as a role.

Every individual did their job. The checkmark still went green, and the green was a kind of lie that no single person told. It was assembled out of a correct harness, a faithful test run, a stale dataset, and a missing owner, each of which looked fine in isolation. That is what a seam failure looks like: one role produces a signal, other roles rely on it without inspecting it, and the failure lives in the hand-off nobody believed was theirs to check.

On a team that has actually closed this seam, a green eval is not finished the moment the suite passes. It is finished when the eval owner can state the pass rate across repeated runs, the judge's calibration, and the coverage gaps, and the domain expert has signed that the dataset still reflects the current rules, and only then does the product manager read the green checkmark as meaning what the release meeting needs it to mean.

What travels with the checkmark

There is one discipline that binds the four roles together, and it belongs to the eval owner to enforce: a passing result has to arrive with its provenance attached, not just its color.

A checkmark that shows up as a single green light has its chain of custody hidden, and a hidden chain of custody is exactly where a stale threshold survives eleven weeks undetected. A checkmark that arrives with its pass rate across runs, its worst slice, its judge calibration, its coverage gaps, and a domain expert's sign-off is a checkmark the product manager can actually gate a launch on, because every link in that chain has a name attached to it.

The difference between those two checkmarks is invisible in the room where the ship decision gets made. Both render as the same shade of green on the same dashboard. It becomes visible weeks later, when someone in compliance, or audit, or the field reads an output closely enough to notice what the suite never actually checked. When that happens, the question "who signed off on this" needs an answer that already exists, not one assembled after the fact under pressure.

What the PM keeps, and what moves

The product manager does not lose the gate. Deciding what success means, what tradeoffs the product accepts, and whether to ship on a given day is still squarely a PM responsibility, and nothing about a four-owner model changes that.

What moves is the assumption that the PM can personally vouch for the number behind the gate. Keeping the golden dataset honest as the world changes around it is not a PM skill, and it was never really an engineering skill either; it needed a name and an owner of its own, which is what the eval owner role is for. And knowing whether a specific answer is still correct in a specific domain, whether a threshold, a guideline, or a policy quietly moved out from under the agent, is not something evaluation engineering can manufacture no matter how well the harness is built. It has to come from someone who lives in that domain and is accountable for saying when the ground has shifted.

Take the agent your team is closest to shipping and find the one eval number everyone in the room already trusts. Ask, of specific people rather than the dashboard: who keeps the reference data current, who calibrated the judge, who signed that the answers it grades against are still correct today, and what it would look like for that number to stay green while the rule underneath it has already changed. If the answer to any of those is that no one owns it, the suite is not the problem. The gap between the roles is, and it is still open.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how a passing test result depends on ownership the traditional software team never assigned. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Employee You Never Onboarded
entry.content:
The Employee You Never Onboarded

Post 10, The Agentic AI Team series

A company stands up an agent that triages inbound support tickets, drafts responses, and escalates the hard cases to a person. Procurement logged it as a software purchase. IT logged it as an integration. Nobody in the people function heard about it, because that function is for people, and this was a subscription. Six months in, the agent handles a quarter of all ticket volume, its behavior has drifted twice after silent model updates nobody noticed, and when a customer complaint forces the question of who has been watching it, the honest answer is no one. Not because the team is careless. Because the job was never written down anywhere a person could be assigned to it.

That gap is not a bug in this rollout. It is what happens every time an organization adds an actor with its own judgment and processes the addition as a purchase instead of a hire.

An actor with judgment, and no file on it

When an organization adds a person to a team, an apparatus switches on without anyone deciding to switch it on. The person is interviewed against the role. Someone checks references, which is really someone asking whether this person's behavior under pressure matches their resume. They are onboarded: told what they own, what they may not touch, who to escalate to. They have a manager who notices when their work drifts, and they can be coached, reassigned, or let go, with a record of why. None of this feels like infrastructure, because it is old enough to be invisible. It is a century of organizational practice built to answer one question: how do you put an actor with its own judgment into a system and stay accountable for what it does.

An agent goes through none of it. There is no interview, because the procurement category was software license, not hire. There is no onboarding beyond a system prompt written in an afternoon. There is no manager, because managing it is in no one's job description. There is no termination clause, because you do not fire a subscription. The apparatus that would have caught a human's problems was never pointed at it.

Call this anthropomorphizing and the objection deserves a straight answer. The claim is not that the agent thinks the way a person thinks. The claim is that the org chart cannot tell the difference, because the apparatus exists for an actor that takes action, not one that is conscious. If a system decides inside a scope of authority and hands results to people who treat them as input to their own work, it behaves like a team member whatever is or is not happening under the hood. Call it what you actually have: a contractor with broad system access, no statement of work, and no way to end the engagement.

That is structural, not a lapse by an otherwise careful organization. An agent fits no slot the org chart already has. It is not an employee: no contract, no review cycle, no manager. It is not a vendor: no sales representative answers for its behavior, and its behavior changes without a release anyone approved. It is not a tool in the old sense, since a tool does what it is told and this one decides. It sits between the categories, and none of the governance built for any of them applies.

What onboarding, a manager, and an exit mean at the org level

That gap will not close on its own, and it takes a different shape at the org level than on a single engineering team. Two supervisory seats are the minimum answer. One is the agent supervisor, sometimes folded into an "AgentOps" function, whose job is to watch what the running agent is doing right now and notice when its behavior changes. The other is the eval owner, who keeps answering whether the agent is still correct, not just whether it is still running. Neither seat existed eighteen months ago. Both now do, at the organizational level, what a manager and a performance review already do for a human hire.

Onboarding, at this level, is a decision about scope before the agent ever runs: what it may do, what it may never touch, what happens the moment it hits a case outside that scope. It is a product and risk decision, shared by whoever owns the product and whoever owns security, and it has to exist before deployment, not get inferred from an incident afterward.

A manager, at this level, is not a person reading the agent's output line by line. It is the agent supervisor or AgentOps seat, whose actual week includes watching the fleet: how often it escalates, how often a human overrides it, whether its behavior this month resembles last month's. That watching does not stop, because the agent does not hold still, and a supervisor who checked once at launch has inspected a snapshot of an actor that kept moving after the inspection ended.

A decommission plan, at this level, answers a question most organizations cannot currently answer: if this agent had to be pulled tomorrow, who revokes its access, and who tells the people who relied on it. A human's departure has a checklist. An agent's usually does not.

None of this is the developer's daily review loop. A team that has solved code review well can still have zero of it in place for the agent handling customer refunds, a different agent with no engineer reading its output as unforgiving, testable code. The org-level apparatus has to exist on its own.

Staffing the seats by stage

Here the honest answer stops being one org chart and depends on size. A startup that separates every one of these responsibilities into its own hire drowns before it ships anything. An enterprise that lets one person hold the entire supervisory function for every agent it runs is one incident from finding out that person had no time to do the job. The organizing idea fits one sentence: merge to decide, separate to ship. Deciding what to build can concentrate in a small group. Watching what is now running in production cannot, once there is enough of it to watch.

At a startup, one senior person can reasonably hold most of the build side of an agent: what it does, how it is structured, how it ships. That concentration is correct at this size, not a shortcut. What cannot be skipped, even at five people, is a second pair of eyes on whether the agent can be trusted, separate from whoever built it, since the builder is the worst-positioned person to catch their own agent's blind spot, for the same reason code review exists at all.

At a scale-up, these responsibilities stop being part of someone's week and become someone's actual job. An agent supervisor emerges because the running fleet has grown past what anyone can watch on the side. An eval owner emerges because keeping a golden dataset current is more work than a generalist can absorb between other duties. The product lead, who used to make every call about scope and trust, now shares that territory with people who can see drift and correctness alone. This is where organizations grow well or stumble, and the difference is whether the new seat is framed as work that finally has an owner, or as authority someone took away. Naming the work, not the rank, keeps it from becoming a turf fight.

At enterprise scale, the seats become teams, and a failure shows up that smaller organizations never encounter: the hand-off between teams becomes an organizational boundary instead of a conversation down the hall. The supervisor who used to have a direct line to the product lead becomes an AgentOps function reachable now by ticket, and a ticket carries intent worse than a conversation does. The biggest risk at this scale is rarely an unstaffed seat. It is an unowned seam between two fully staffed ones.

This supervisory layer's newness shows up in compensation. Product roles focused on building AI capability now command some of the highest pay in the industry, because the market has decided the judgment of what to build is scarce, and it is right about that. Look for an established compensation band for the agent supervisor or the standing eval owner, and it is largely absent: a well-priced market for deciding what the agent should do, a thin one for watching whether it still does it. That is the empty column from earlier in this series, showing up in the salary survey instead of the org chart. Organizations that staff these seats now are buying something scarce before the market prices it. The ones that wait will pay later, at a premium, under duress.

The box that grows with the work

Picture a small team that starts, sensibly, as three people in a room: someone who owns the product decision, someone who owns the technical structure, someone who owns whether outputs can be trusted. Call it a three-in-a-box. At launch, with one agent handling one workflow, three people can hold the whole thing between them, meeting twice a week, and that is the right size for the job in front of them.

Add a second agent, then a third, spread across the business, and the box under strain looks different. It does not disappear. It becomes the seed that three or four boxes grow from, one trio per agent, each still merging build decisions the way the original did, each also owning a staffed answer to who is watching this agent today. Scale further, to an enterprise running agents across dozens of processes, and the boxes consolidate into functions: a central AgentOps team every trio feeds into, a standing evaluation function every trio draws on rather than rebuilding its own. The three-in-a-box was never the wrong shape, only the right shape for the size the organization was at the time, and the mistake is assuming the shape that worked at five people still works at five hundred.

The question with no easy exit

So take the most important agent your organization runs and ask the questions you would ask about a new hire in a consequential seat. Who onboarded it, and into what scope. Who is its manager, in the sense of someone whose actual job includes noticing when it drifts. Who would revoke its access tomorrow if it had to be pulled, and who would tell the people relying on it. For a person in that seat, every one of those questions has a name attached. For most agents already running in production, they do not, and the discomfort of asking out loud is the most accurate measure of how much of the hiring got skipped.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the organization staffs the supervisory seats an agent requires, from a single person wearing every hat at a startup to named functions at enterprise scale. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: Re: Employee History Import Rule in Variable Pay with MDF...
entry.content: @rebeccanussba It's the start date of the jobInfo or compInfo record that's effective as of the varpay program start date that you defined in Plan Setup > Settings. There is a setting that enables us to override this to show the program start date on the worksheets but in the employee history it can't be fixed and will always be that jobInfo record start date (and I like it better like that because it does make troubleshooting easier sometimes).
